summary: Dan Luu catalogs significant errors from his blog posts between 2013-2024, classifying them into categories like insufficient thought, naivete, insufficient verification, blunders, and confusing explanations. He draws a subjective line between 'serious' errors that materially affected his arguments versus minor typos, noting that many major errors came from his first three months of blogging. The post exemplifies his commitment to transparency and intellectual honesty, treating corrections as an ongoing process rather than a one-time exercise.
key_points: Many major errors occurred during the first three months of blogging in 2013
Errors range from insufficient thought and naivete about software to arithmetic blunders and misreading sources
The classification system is subjective and categories aren't mutually exclusive
Some errors stemmed from rushing last-minute edits before publication
The list is expected to grow over time as new errors are discovered or made
money_quotes: the difference isn't that those companies don't produce bad (unreliable, buggy, slow, etc.) software, it's that product/market fit and network effects are important enough that it frequently doesn't matter that software is bad
A list that included everything, including typos would both be uninteresting for other people to read as well as high overhead for me

I distinctly remember not being sure what nostaglebraist was saying and originally agreed with the correct interpretation. After re-reading it, I came away with my mistaken reading, which I then wrote into my post. That I had uncertainty about the reading should've caused me to just reproduce his analysis
I view it as a good sign that a large fraction of the list is from my first three months of blogging, in 2013, but that's no reason to get complacent
key_insight: Maintaining a public corrections log demonstrates intellectual honesty while revealing how many errors stem not from lack of knowledge but from rushing under time pressure or failing to verify when uncertainty exists.
